mnemonics: CLAP: Confidence Limits Are Probable. Reminds you that the limits define a range where the true population parameter is probably located, not a probability about the parameter itself.

The Coffee Mug: Imagine a mug. The mug (the sample) is a fixed size, but the coffee (the confidence interval) sloshes around inside it. 95% of the time, when you pour a new mug (take a new sample), the coffee (the interval) will contain the true average caffeine content (the parameter).
keyPoints: A confidence interval provides a range of values, derived from a sample statistic, that is likely to contain an unknown population parameter.

The confidence level (e.g., 95%) refers to the long-run success rate of the method: if you were to take many samples and build an interval from each, 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter.
The interval itself is fixed once calculated from a specific sample; the probability is about the method, not the specific interval.
commonMistakes: Interpreting a 95% confidence interval as meaning 'there is a 95% probability that the true parameter is inside this specific interval.' The parameter is fixed; the interval is random.
Believing the confidence level is a property of the single interval itself, rather than the process used to create it.
Using the logic 'The parameter must be in the interval because the method works 95% of the time' for a single, already-computed interval.
